title: CLAUDE.md
text: milt (Modern Integrated Library Timeseries) CRAN-ready R package providing unified API time series forecasting, anomaly detection, classification, clustering R. Think Python’s Darts/sktime, natively R tidyverse-friendly API. Function prefix: milt_ Class prefix: Milt (e.g., MiltSeries, MiltModel, MiltForecast) Requires R >= 4.1.0 (native pipe |> support) Uses renv dependency management

title: CLAUDE.md
text: commands run within R (RStudio terminal):
code: # Load all package code (primary dev workflow) devtools::load_all()  # Regenerate NAMESPACE and man/ from roxygen2 docs devtools::document()  # Run full R CMD check (CRAN compliance) devtools::check()  # Run all tests devtools::test()  # Run a single test file testthat::test_file("tests/testthat/test-MiltSeries.R")  # Install package locally devtools::install()

title: CLAUDE.md
text: package follows strict 4-layer architecture: Key design rule — S3 outside, R6 inside: Users interact via S3 generics (print, plot, summary). R6 classes manage internal state. Users never call $methods() directly.
code: Layer 1: User-facing API     — S3 generics + exported functions Layer 2: Core Engine         — R6 classes (MiltSeriesR6, ModelRegistry, etc.) Layer 3: Model Backends      — Isolated adapter files (one backend per file) Layer 4: Data Layer          — tsibble, arrow, data.table integration

title: CLAUDE.md
text: MiltSeriesR6 (R/MiltSeries.R) — foundational data class; everything else depends . Wraps tibble time column, value column(s), frequency, optional covariates. MiltForecast (R/MiltForecast.R) — result milt_forecast() MiltAnomalies (R/MiltAnomalies.R) — result milt_detect()

title: CLAUDE.md
text: Every model (classical, ML, deep learning) follows pattern: Model backends live isolated files (R/backend-*.R). Adding new model requires creating one backend file registering — changes core code.

title: CLAUDE.md
text: Imports (always available): R6, rlang, cli, glue, vctrs, tibble, dplyr, tidyr, lubridate, tsibble, ggplot2, data.table Suggests (lazy-loaded demand): forecast, fable, prophet, xgboost, lightgbm, torch, ranger, glmnet, shiny, plumber, etc. Use rlang::check_installed() (via internal check_installed_backend() helper) load Suggests packages helpful error message missing.

title: CLAUDE.md
text: user-facing messages use cli package: milt_info(), milt_warn(), milt_abort() (R/utils-cli.R). Use inline markup: {.var x}, {.fn milt_fit}, {.val 12}. Every exported function must roxygen2 docs description, params, @return, least one @examples block, @seealso cross-references. Pipe-friendly: every user-facing function takes data/object first argument returns something pipeable. Fail loudly: errors must say went wrong fix . Tests: testthat edition 3 (Config/testthat/edition: 3). One test file per source file (tests/testthat/test-<source-name>.R).

title: Anomaly Detection with milt
text: milt’s anomaly detection API follows two-step pattern: Results returned MiltAnomalies object print(), plot(), as_tibble() methods.

what: 1. Create a series with injected spikes
title: Anomaly Detection with milt
code: set.seed(42) tbl <- tibble::tibble(   date  = seq(as.Date("2020-01-01"), by = "month", length.out = 60),   value = c(rnorm(20, 10, 1), 50, rnorm(19, 10, 1),   # spike at 21              rnorm(10, 10, 1), -30, rnorm(9, 10, 1))   # dip at 51 ) s <- milt_series(tbl, time_col = "date", value_cols = "value") plot(s)

title: Anomaly Detection with milt
code: d   <- milt_detector("iqr", k = 1.5) anm <- milt_detect(d, s) print(anm) #> # MiltAnomalies [iqr] #> # Series    : 60 observations  2020-01-01 — 2024-12-01 #> # Anomalies : 3 / 60 (5%)# Anomalous times: #> # A tibble: 3 × 3 #>   time        value .anomaly_score #>   <date>      <dbl>          <dbl> #> 1 2021-06-01   7.34         0.0957 #> 2 2021-09-01  50           29.3    #> 3 2024-03-01 -30           29.3 plot(anm)

title: Anomaly Detection with milt
code: d_gesd <- milt_detector("gesd", max_anoms = 5, alpha = 0.05) anm_gesd <- milt_detect(d_gesd, s) plot(anm_gesd)

title: Anomaly Detection with milt
text: STL separates trend seasonality; anomalies remainder:
code: air <- milt_series(AirPassengers) d_stl  <- milt_detector("stl", threshold = 3) anm_stl <- milt_detect(d_stl, air) print(anm_stl) #> # MiltAnomalies [stl] #> # Series    : 144 observations  1949-01-01 — 1960-12-01 #> # Anomalies : 6 / 144 (4.2%)# Anomalous times: #> # A tibble: 6 × 3 #>   time       value .anomaly_score #>   <date>     <dbl>          <dbl> #> 1 1958-07-01   491           3.17 #> 2 1958-08-01   505           3.36 #> 3 1959-07-01   548           3.54 #> 4 1959-08-01   559           3.53 #> 5 1960-07-01   622           4.39 #> 6 1960-08-01   606           3.44 plot(anm_stl)

title: Anomaly Detection with milt
text: Requires isotree package:
code: d_if  <- milt_detector("iforest", n_trees = 50, threshold = 0.6) anm_if <- milt_detect(d_if, s) plot(anm_if)

title: Anomaly Detection with milt
text: Requires dbscan package:
code: d_lof  <- milt_detector("lof", k = 5, threshold = 1.5) anm_lof <- milt_detect(d_lof, s) plot(anm_lof)

title: Anomaly Detection with milt
text: Combine multiple detectors majority vote mean score:
code: d1  <- milt_detector("iqr",   k = 1.5) d2  <- milt_detector("grubbs", alpha = 0.05) d3  <- milt_detector("gesd",  max_anoms = 5)  ens <- milt_detector("ensemble",                       detectors = list(d1, d2, d3),                       method    = "majority") anm_ens <- milt_detect(ens, s) plot(anm_ens)

title: Deep Learning Models with milt
text: milt ships six deep learning backends built torch R. follow milt_model() |> milt_fit() |> milt_forecast() pattern every model. Note: DL examples vignette run automatically package checks (eval = FALSE). Install torch run interactively.

title: Deep Learning Models with milt
text: Neural basis expansion interpretable time series forecasting:
code: air <- milt_series(AirPassengers)  fct_nbeats <- milt_model("nbeats",                            input_chunk_length  = 24,                            output_chunk_length = 12,                            n_epochs            = 20) |>   milt_fit(air) |>   milt_forecast(12)  plot(fct_nbeats)

title: Deep Learning Models with milt
text: Multi-rate hierarchical interpolation:
code: fct_nhits <- milt_model("nhits",                           input_chunk_length  = 24,                           output_chunk_length = 12,                           n_stacks            = 3,                           n_epochs            = 20) |>   milt_fit(air) |>   milt_forecast(12)  plot(fct_nhits)

title: Deep Learning Models with milt
text: Dilated causal convolutions residual connections:
code: fct_tcn <- milt_model("tcn",                         n_filters   = 32,                         kernel_size = 3,                         n_layers    = 4,                         n_epochs    = 30) |>   milt_fit(air) |>   milt_forecast(12)  plot(fct_tcn)

title: Deep Learning Models with milt
text: LSTM encoder Gaussian likelihood — returns true predictive distributions:
code: fct_deepar <- milt_model("deepar",                            n_epochs     = 30,                            hidden_size  = 32) |>   milt_fit(air) |>   milt_forecast(12)  # Inspect prediction intervals head(fct_deepar$as_tibble()) plot(fct_deepar)

title: Deep Learning Models with milt
text: LSTM encoder + cross-attention decoder:
code: fct_tft <- milt_model("tft",                         input_chunk_length  = 24,                         output_chunk_length = 12,                         hidden_size         = 32,                         n_epochs            = 20) |>   milt_fit(air) |>   milt_forecast(12)  plot(fct_tft)

title: Deep Learning Models with milt
text: Transformer patch-based tokenisation:
code: fct_pt <- milt_model("patch_tst",                        input_chunk_length  = 48,                        output_chunk_length = 12,                        patch_len           = 8,                        n_epochs            = 20) |>   milt_fit(air) |>   milt_forecast(12)  plot(fct_pt)

title: Deploying milt Models
text: model trained can : 1. Saved disk milt_save() restored milt_load(). 2. Served REST API milt_serve() (Plumber). 3. Explored dashboard milt_dashboard() (Shiny). 4. Included report milt_report() (R Markdown / Quarto).

title: Deploying milt Models
text: milt object can saved: MiltSeries, MiltForecast, MiltAnomalies, detectors, etc.
code: air <- milt_series(AirPassengers) m   <- milt_model("ets") |> milt_fit(air)  # Save path <- milt_save(m, "my_ets_model")   # writes my_ets_model.milt cat("Saved to:", path, "\n")  # Load later m2 <- milt_load("my_ets_model.milt") fct <- milt_forecast(m2, 12) plot(fct)

title: Deploying milt Models
text: milt_serve() wraps model Plumber API exposes: Test curl:
code: # Launch (blocking — runs until interrupted) milt_serve(m, host = "127.0.0.1", port = 8000)  # Non-blocking: get the router object without starting router <- milt_serve(m, launch = FALSE) curl -s http://127.0.0.1:8000/health curl -s -X POST http://127.0.0.1:8000/forecast \      -H "Content-Type: application/json" \      -d '{"horizon": 12}'

title: Deploying milt Models
text: milt_dashboard() launches interactive four-tab app: Series — interactive line chart training data. Forecast — adjustable horizon slider PI fan chart. Diagnostics — residuals, ACF plot, histogram. Anomalies — IQR anomaly overlay adjustable multiplier.

title: Deploying milt Models
text: milt_report() renders self-contained HTML PDF analysis report: report includes: - Series overview plot - Diagnostic tests (ADF, KPSS, seasonality) - EDA statistics - Forecast plots supplied model - Anomaly detection overview
code: # HTML (default) path <- milt_report(   series  = air,   models  = list(ets = m),   horizon = 24 ) cat("Report at:", path, "\n")  # PDF milt_report(air, output_format = "pdf", output_file = "air_report.pdf")

title: Deploying milt Models
text: Save checkpoints refit:
code: # After initial training milt_save(m, "model_v1")  # After refit on new data new_data <- milt_series(AirPassengers) m_v2     <- milt_refit(m, new_data) milt_save(m_v2, "model_v2")  # Load the best version best <- milt_load("model_v1.milt")

title: EDA and Diagnostics
code: library(milt)  ap <- milt_series(AirPassengers)  eda  <- milt_eda(ap) diag <- milt_diagnose(ap)  print(eda) print(diag)

title: EDA and Diagnostics
text: Returns: MiltEDA object. Use print(), summary(), tibble::as_tibble() inspect results.

title: EDA and Diagnostics
text: EDA report starts table basic statistics: Reported statistics include: Extract tibble downstream use:

title: EDA and Diagnostics
text: milt automatically runs two complementary stationarity tests: tests always computed using base-R implementations (extra packages required). Interpreting combined result: Access raw test results:
code: stat <- eda$stationarity()  stat$pp_pvalue    # Phillips-Perron p-value stat$kpss_pvalue  # KPSS p-value stat$stationary   # logical: TRUE if both tests agree series is stationary

title: EDA and Diagnostics
text: Seasonal strength computed via STL decomposition (stats::stl(s.window = "periodic", robust = TRUE)): strength=max(0,1−Var(remainder)Var(seasonal)+Var(remainder))\text{strength} = \max\!\left(0,\ 1 - \frac{\text{Var(remainder)}}{\text{Var(seasonal)} + \text{Var(remainder)}}\right) Values range 0 (seasonality) 1 (perfectly seasonal). strength 0.40 generally considered meaningful modelling purposes.
code: seas <- eda$seasonality()  seas$period    # dominant seasonal period (e.g. 12 for monthly data) seas$strength  # strength in [0, 1] seas$seasonal  # logical: TRUE if strength > 0.40

title: EDA and Diagnostics
text: plots = TRUE, milt_eda() renders following plots: ACF / PACF — generated milt_plot_acf(): Useful identifying: - Slow ACF decay → non-stationary trend present. - Spikes lag m → seasonality period m. - Significant PACF lag 1–2 → AR process. Decomposition — generated milt_plot_decomp(): Decomposes series trend, seasonal, remainder components using STL. Helpful understanding dominant patterns choosing model. Distribution — histogram + density overlay value distribution. Helps identify skewness, heavy tails, bimodality may warrant transformations (e.g. log, Box-Cox).

title: EDA and Diagnostics
code: diag <- milt_diagnose(ap) print(diag)  # MiltDiagnosis # Series    : 144 obs @ monthly # Range     : 1949-01-01 — 1960-12-01 # # Stationarity : non-stationary (CV ratio = 0.412) # Seasonality  : seasonal (strength = 0.92, period = 12) # Trend        : trend present (slope = 2.667, p = 3.1e-52) # Gaps         : 0 # Outliers     : 0 # # Recommendations: #  • Consider differencing or a model with trend/drift. #  • Use a seasonal model (snaive, ETS, SARIMA, TBATS, Prophet).

title: EDA and Diagnostics
text: Reading ACF: Slow geometric decay (lags positive declining slowly) → strong trend; series needs differencing. Sine-wave pattern → deterministic seasonality. Single significant spike lag k, cuts → MA(k) process. lags inside confidence bands → white noise (autocorrelation). Reading PACF: Single significant spike lag 1 → AR(1) process. Significant spikes lags 1–p, cuts → AR(p) process. Spike seasonal lag m → seasonal AR term.
code: milt_plot_acf(series, lags = NULL, type = c("both", "acf", "pacf")) # Side-by-side ACF and PACF milt_plot_acf(ap, lags = 36, type = "both")  # ACF only with custom lag count milt_plot_acf(ap, lags = 24, type = "acf")

title: EDA and Diagnostics
text: Returns: ggplot2 object four panels: observed, trend, seasonal, remainder.
code: milt_plot_decomp(series, method = c("stl", "classical"), robust = TRUE) milt_plot_decomp(ap)  # Inspect the STL remainder for anomalies milt_plot_decomp(ap, method = "stl", robust = TRUE)

title: EDA and Diagnostics
text: recommended workflow fitting model:
code: library(milt)  # 1. Load data ap <- milt_series(AirPassengers)  # 2. Diagnose structure diag <- milt_diagnose(ap) print(diag)  # 3. Deep-dive EDA eda <- milt_eda(ap) print(eda)  # 4. Visualise autocorrelation structure milt_plot_acf(ap, lags = 36)  # 5. Decompose milt_plot_decomp(ap)  # 6. Choose model based on findings # Strong seasonality + trend → ETS, SARIMA, or Prophet fc <- milt_model("auto_arima") |>   milt_fit(ap) |>   milt_forecast(h = 24)  plot(fc)

code: spl <- milt_split(air, ratio = 0.8) h   <- spl$test$n_timesteps()  fct_cv <- milt_model("ets") |>   milt_fit(spl$train) |>   milt_forecast(h) #> Fitting <MiltEts> model… #> Done in 0.58s.  milt_accuracy(spl$test$values(), fct_cv$as_tibble()$.mean) #> # A tibble: 5 × 2 #>   metric     value #>   <chr>      <dbl> #> 1 MAE      30.0    #> 2 MSE    1282.     #> 3 RMSE     35.8    #> 4 MAPE      0.0653 #> 5 R2        0.790

title: Forecasting with milt
text: Combine forecasts mean ensemble:
code: m1 <- milt_model("auto_arima") |> milt_fit(air) #> Fitting <MiltAutoArima> model… #> Done in 1.23s. m2 <- milt_model("ets")        |> milt_fit(air) #> Fitting <MiltEts> model… #> Done in 0.63s. m3 <- milt_model("naive")      |> milt_fit(air) #> Fitting <MiltNaive> model… #> Done in 0s.  ens <- milt_ensemble(list(arima = m1, ets = m2, naive = m3), method = "mean") fct_ens <- milt_forecast(ens, 12) plot(fct_ens)

title: Forecasting with milt
text: Every model returns 80% 95% prediction intervals. Access via as_tibble(): sample-path–based probabilistic forecasts, use num_samples:
code: tbl <- fct_arima$as_tibble() head(tbl) #> # A tibble: 6 × 7 #>   time       .model     .mean .lower_80 .upper_80 .lower_95 .upper_95 #>   <date>     <chr>      <dbl>     <dbl>     <dbl>     <dbl>     <dbl> #> 1 1961-01-01 auto_arima  446.      431.      460.      423.      468. #> 2 1961-02-01 auto_arima  420.      403.      438.      394.      447. #> 3 1961-03-01 auto_arima  449.      430.      469.      419.      479. #> 4 1961-04-01 auto_arima  492.      471.      513.      460.      524. #> 5 1961-05-01 auto_arima  503.      482.      525.      470.      537. #> 6 1961-06-01 auto_arima  567.      544.      589.      532.      601. fct_samples <- milt_model("naive") |>   milt_fit(air) |>   milt_forecast(12, num_samples = 200)

title: Forecasting with milt
text: milt_backtest() implements expanding- sliding-window CV:
code: bt <- milt_backtest(   milt_model("ets"),   series  = air,   horizon = 12,   stride  = 12,   method  = "expanding" ) #> Running expanding backtest (6 folds): ets, h=12 print(bt) #>  #> ── MiltBacktest <ets> ── #>  #> • Method : expanding #> • Horizon : 12 #> • Folds : 6 #>  #> ── Summary (across folds) #> # A tibble: 3 × 5 #>   metric    mean      sd     min     max #>   <chr>    <dbl>   <dbl>   <dbl>   <dbl> #> 1 MAE    23.8    10.5    13.8    43.5    #> 2 RMSE   28.5    11.9    16.8    50.8    #> 3 MAPE    0.0606  0.0243  0.0396  0.0967 plot(bt)

title: Getting Started with milt
text: milt (Modern Integrated Library Timeseries) provides single, consistent API every time series task: forecasting, anomaly detection, classification, clustering. Every model follows three-step pipe: vignette walks core workflow using built-AirPassengers dataset.

title: Getting Started with milt
text: milt_series() accepts virtually R time series object: ts, data.frame, tibble, xts, tsibble, plain numeric vector. can also build series tibble control every option: Key accessors MiltSeries:

title: Getting Started with milt
text: milt_diagnose() runs quick statistical battery: trend test, stationarity check, seasonality strength, outlier count.
code: dx <- milt_diagnose(air) print(dx) #> # MiltDiagnosis #> # Series    : 144 obs @ monthly# Range     : 1949-01-01 — 1960-12-01 #> Stationarity : stationary (CV ratio = 0.028)Seasonality  : seasonal (strength = 0.462, period = 12)Trend        : trend present (slope = 2.6572, p = 0)Gaps         : noneOutliers     : none #> Recommendations: #> • Significant linear trend detected. Models without detrending may underfit.• Seasonality detected (strength = 0.46, period = 12). Use a seasonal model (ETS, SARIMA, STL).

title: Getting Started with milt
text: Use milt_model() select model name, milt_fit() train , milt_forecast() produce point forecasts plus 80% 95% prediction intervals. result MiltForecast object. Convert tibble downstream work: Plot forecast history:

title: Getting Started with milt
text: Swap "naive" registered key. Models backed forecast package (ETS, ARIMA, Theta, STL) loaded lazily require forecast package installed.
code: list_milt_models() #> # A tibble: 25 × 6 #>    name           description multivariate probabilistic covariates multi_series #>    <chr>          <chr>       <lgl>        <lgl>         <lgl>      <lgl>        #>  1 snaive         "Seasonal … FALSE        TRUE          FALSE      FALSE        #>  2 ets            "Exponenti… FALSE        TRUE          FALSE      FALSE        #>  3 nbeats         ""          FALSE        FALSE         FALSE      FALSE        #>  4 auto_arima     "Automatic… FALSE        TRUE          TRUE       FALSE        #>  5 knn            "K-Nearest… FALSE        TRUE          FALSE      FALSE        #>  6 svm            "Support V… FALSE        TRUE          FALSE      FALSE        #>  7 stl            "STL decom… FALSE        TRUE          FALSE      FALSE        #>  8 elastic_net    ""          FALSE        FALSE         FALSE      FALSE        #>  9 deepar         ""          FALSE        FALSE         FALSE      FALSE        #> 10 darts_transfo… ""          FALSE        FALSE         FALSE      FALSE        #> # ℹ 15 more rows fct_ets <- milt_model("ets") |>   milt_fit(air) |>   milt_forecast(horizon = 24) #> Fitting <MiltEts> model… #> Done in 0.62s.  plot(fct_ets)

title: Getting Started with milt
text: Split series training test sets, forecast test horizon, compute accuracy metrics:
code: spl <- milt_split(air, ratio = 0.8)   # 80 % train, 20 % test  fct_test <- milt_model("naive") |>   milt_fit(spl$train) |>   milt_forecast(spl$test$n_timesteps()) #> Fitting <MiltNaive> model… #> Done in 0s.  acc <- milt_accuracy(   actual    = spl$test$values(),   predicted = fct_test$as_tibble()$.mean ) print(acc) #> # A tibble: 5 × 2 #>   metric    value #>   <chr>     <dbl> #> 1 MAE      81.4   #> 2 MSE    8674.    #> 3 RMSE     93.1   #> 4 MAPE      0.202 #> 5 R2       -0.421

title: Getting Started with milt
text: milt_backtest() performs time series cross-validation without data leakage. Two strategies supported: expanding (default) — training window grows fold fold. sliding — fixed-size training window advances stride steps.  Retrieve per-fold results tibble:
code: bt <- milt_backtest(   model          = milt_model("naive"),   series         = air,   horizon        = 12,   initial_window = 108L,   stride         = 12L,   metrics        = c("MAE", "RMSE") ) #> Running expanding backtest (3 folds): naive, h=12  print(bt) #>  #> ── MiltBacktest <naive> ── #>  #> • Method : expanding #> • Horizon : 12 #> • Folds : 3 #>  #> ── Summary (across folds) #> # A tibble: 2 × 5 #>   metric  mean    sd   min   max #>   <chr>  <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> #> 1 MAE     73.2  19.6  52.3  91.3 #> 2 RMSE    97.5  19.0  76.4 113. plot(bt) tibble::as_tibble(bt) #> # A tibble: 3 × 5 #>   .fold .train_n .test_n  .MAE .RMSE #>   <int>    <int>   <int> <dbl> <dbl> #> 1     1      108      12  52.3  76.4 #> 2     2      120      12  91.3 113.  #> 3     3      132      12  76   103.

title: Getting Started with milt
text: imputation methods: "spline", "locf", "nocb", "mean", "zero".
code: # Detect and fill gaps with linear interpolation air_gapped <- air   # hypothetical series with missing values air_filled <- milt_fill_gaps(air_gapped, method = "linear")

title: Getting Started with milt
code: # Subset to 1955-1960 air_55 <- milt_window(air,                       start = as.Date("1955-01-01"),                       end   = as.Date("1960-12-01")) air_55$n_timesteps() #> [1] 72  # Head / tail milt_head(air, 12)$n_timesteps() #> [1] 12 milt_tail(air, 12)$n_timesteps() #> [1] 12

title: Getting Started with milt
text: See vignette("milt-backends") complete model catalogue (coming soon). See vignette("milt-covariates") covariate-aware forecasting. Visit https://github.com/ntiGideon/milt follow development open issue.

title: Forecast Accuracy Metrics Guide
text: point metrics share signature (actual, predicted) arguments numeric vectors length.

title: Forecast Accuracy Metrics Guide
text: MAE=1n∑t=1n|yt−ŷt|\text{MAE} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{t=1}^{n} |y_t - \hat{y}_t| Returns: single non-negative number units actual. Interpretation: average absolute deviation observed predicted values. MAE 50 means forecasts 50 units average. use: want easily interpretable, outlier-robust error measure single series scale comparisons meaningful. Weakness: comparable across series different scales units.
code: library(milt)  actual    <- c(100, 110, 95, 130, 120) predicted <- c(105, 108, 98, 125, 118)  milt_mae(actual, predicted) #> [1] 4.6

title: Forecast Accuracy Metrics Guide
text: MSE=1n∑t=1n(yt−ŷt)2\text{MSE} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{t=1}^{n} (y_t - \hat{y}_t)^2 Returns: single non-negative number squared units. Interpretation: MSE penalises large errors heavily MAE. model occasionally produces huge errors much higher MSE one consistently small errors. use: large errors disproportionately costly (e.g. energy trading, manufacturing). Weakness: squared units make hard interpret directly; scale-free.

title: Forecast Accuracy Metrics Guide
text: RMSE=MSE\text{RMSE} = \sqrt{\text{MSE}} Returns: single non-negative number units actual. Interpretation: RMSE restores original scale retaining MSE’s property penalising large errors heavily. widely reported metric academic forecasting competitions. use: want scale-preserved error still penalises large mistakes.

title: Forecast Accuracy Metrics Guide
text: MAPE=1n∑t=1n|yt−ŷtyt|\text{MAPE} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{t=1}^{n} \left|\frac{y_t - \hat{y}_t}{y_t}\right| Returns: single numeric value (expressed fraction, percentage — multiply 100 display). value 0.05 means 5 %. Interpretation: percentage error, making comparable across series different scales. Widely used industry. use: actuals always strictly positive want scale-free, easily explainable metric. Weaknesses: - Undefined (returns Inf) actual value zero. - Asymmetric: absolute error produces larger percentage actual small vs. large. - Biased towards forecasts low.
code: milt_mape(actual, predicted) #> [1] 0.04085 # i.e. ≈ 4.1 %  # When actuals contain zero, use milt_smape() instead: actuals_with_zero <- c(0, 10, 20, 30) milt_smape(actuals_with_zero, c(1, 9, 22, 28))

title: Forecast Accuracy Metrics Guide
text: sMAPE=2n∑t=1n|yt−ŷt||yt|+|ŷt|\text{sMAPE} = \frac{2}{n}\sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{|y_t - \hat{y}_t|}{|y_t| + |\hat{y}_t|} Returns: single numeric value [0, 2] (.e. [0 %, 200 %]). Interpretation: symmetric around - -prediction avoids division zero actual predicted non-zero. value 0.10 means approximately 10 % symmetric error. use: series contain zeros near-zeros, want symmetric, bounded error measure. Weakness: can still undefined actual predicted zero simultaneously (milt sets terms 0 emits warning).

title: Forecast Accuracy Metrics Guide
text: MASE=MAE1n−m∑t=m+1n|yt−yt−m|\text{MASE} = \frac{\text{MAE}}{\frac{1}{n-m}\sum_{t=m+1}^{n}|y_t - y_{t-m}|} m seasonal period. Returns: single non-negative number. Interpretation: - MASE < 1: model outperforms naïve seasonal benchmark. - MASE = 1: model matches naïve forecasting exactly. - MASE > 1: model worse naïve. use: recommended metric comparing forecast accuracy across series different scales seasonal patterns (e.g. M4 competition). works: denominator mean absolute error seasonal naïve forecast training data, providing scale-free, unbiased baseline.
code: milt_mase(actual, predicted, training, season = 1L) train_data <- as.numeric(AirPassengers)[1:120] test_data  <- as.numeric(AirPassengers)[121:144] fc_naive   <- rep(tail(train_data, 12), 2)  # seasonal naïve  milt_mase(test_data, fc_naive, training = train_data, season = 12L) #> [1] 1.0   # exactly naïve, as expected

what: Root Mean Squared Scaled Error — milt_rmsse()
title: Forecast Accuracy Metrics Guide
text: RMSSE=MSE1n−m∑t=m+1n(yt−yt−m)2\text{RMSSE} = \sqrt{\frac{\text{MSE}}{\frac{1}{n-m}\sum_{t=m+1}^{n}(y_t - y_{t-m})^2}} signature milt_mase(). Interpretation: RMSE analogue MASE. Values < 1 indicate better--naive performance. RMSSE penalises large errors MASE. Used primary metric M5 forecasting competition.

title: Forecast Accuracy Metrics Guide
text: MRAE=1n∑t=1n|yt−ŷt||yt−b̂t|\text{MRAE} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{|y_t - \hat{y}_t|}{|y_t - \hat{b}_t|} Interpretation: values < 1 mean model outperforms benchmark.
code: milt_mrae(actual, predicted, benchmark) benchmark <- rep(mean(actual), length(actual)) milt_mrae(actual, predicted, benchmark)

title: Forecast Accuracy Metrics Guide
text: R2=1−∑(yt−ŷt)2∑(yt−y‾)2R^2 = 1 - \frac{\sum(y_t - \hat{y}_t)^2}{\sum(y_t - \bar{y})^2} Returns: numeric value, typically (−∞,1](-\infty, 1]. Interpretation: - R² = 1: perfect fit. - R² = 0: model better predicting mean. - R² < 0: model worse predicting mean. use: supplementary measure explained variance; less informative MASE RMSSE time series.

title: Forecast Accuracy Metrics Guide
text: milt_accuracy() computes multiple metrics fitted model backtest result: Returns: tibble one row per model (series) one column per metric.
code: milt_accuracy(object, metrics = c("MAE", "RMSE", "MAPE", "MASE", "RMSSE")) ap  <- milt_series(AirPassengers) sp  <- milt_split(ap, prop = 0.8)  fc  <- milt_model("auto_arima") |>   milt_fit(sp$train) |>   milt_forecast(h = milt_tail(sp$test, n = nrow(sp$test$data()))$n_timesteps())  milt_accuracy(fc, metrics = c("MAE", "RMSE", "MAPE", "MASE"))

title: Forecast Accuracy Metrics Guide
text: metrics evaluate distributional interval forecasts, model produces uncertainty estimates rather single point forecast.

title: Forecast Accuracy Metrics Guide
text: Formula (energy score form): CRPS=𝔼|X−y|−12𝔼|X−X′|\text{CRPS} = \mathbb{E}|X - y| - \frac{1}{2}\mathbb{E}|X - X'| X,X′X, X' independent draws forecast distribution yy observation. Interpretation: lower better. CRPS 0 perfect (degenerate distribution concentrated true value). CRPS collapses MAE point forecast given. use: comparing probabilistic models (e.g. DeepAR, TFT) evaluating whether uncertainty estimates well-calibrated.
code: milt_crps(actual, forecast_dist) # Simulate 100 sample paths from a simple normal predictive distribution set.seed(42) n_steps  <- 12 n_samples <- 100  actual_vals   <- rnorm(n_steps, mean = 100, sd = 15) forecast_dist <- matrix(rnorm(n_steps * n_samples, mean = 100, sd = 18),                         nrow = n_steps, ncol = n_samples)  milt_crps(actual_vals, forecast_dist)

title: Forecast Accuracy Metrics Guide
text: Formula: Lτ(y,q̂)={τ(y−q̂)y≥q̂(1−τ)(q̂−y)y<q̂L_\tau(y, \hat{q}) = \begin{cases} \tau (y - \hat{q}) & \text{} y \ge \hat{q} \\ (1 - \tau)(\hat{q} - y) & \text{} y < \hat{q} \end{cases} Interpretation: minimising average pinball loss level τ\tau yields optimal τ\tau-quantile forecast. Lower better. use: evaluating quantile forecasts, e.g. assess model’s 10th 90th percentile estimates independently.
code: milt_pinball(actual, quantile_forecast, tau) # Evaluate the 90th-percentile forecast actual_vals      <- c(95, 103, 110, 98, 115, 122, 108) q90_forecast     <- c(100, 110, 112, 105, 118, 120, 115)  milt_pinball(actual_vals, q90_forecast, tau = 0.9)

title: Forecast Accuracy Metrics Guide
text: Formula: Wα={(U−L)+2α(L−y)y<L(U−L)L≤y≤U(U−L)+2α(y−U)y>UW_\alpha = \begin{cases} (U - L) + \frac{2}{\alpha}(L - y) & \text{} y < L \\ (U - L) & \text{} L \le y \le U \\ (U - L) + \frac{2}{\alpha}(y - U) & \text{} y > U \end{cases} Interpretation: score equals interval width observation falls inside; penalty proportional miss distance added falls outside. Narrower intervals rewarded, missing observation incurs large penalty. Lower better. use: evaluating quality model’s prediction intervals jointly (sharpness calibration simultaneously).
code: milt_winkler(actual, lower, upper, alpha) actual_vals <- c(95, 103, 110, 98, 115, 122, 108) lower_80    <- c(82,  90, 95,  86, 100, 105,  93) upper_80    <- c(115, 120, 128, 112, 132, 138, 122)  milt_winkler(actual_vals, lower_80, upper_80, alpha = 0.2)

title: Forecast Accuracy Metrics Guide
text: Formula: Coverage=1n∑t=1n𝟏[Lt≤yt≤Ut]\text{Coverage} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{t=1}^{n} \mathbf{1}[L_t \le y_t \le U_t] Interpretation: fraction observations fall within stated prediction interval. well-calibrated 80 % interval coverage ≈ 0.80. Coverage > nominal = -conservative intervals; coverage < nominal = -conservative (narrow). use: quick calibration check probabilistic model producing prediction intervals.
code: milt_coverage(actual, lower, upper) milt_coverage(actual_vals, lower_80, upper_80) #> [1] 1.0  # all 7 observations within the 80 % interval

title: Forecast Accuracy Metrics Guide
text: milt_backtest() milt_cv() return MiltBacktest objects. Pass milt_accuracy() aggregate metrics across folds:
code: bt <- milt_backtest(   milt_model("auto_arima"),   milt_series(AirPassengers),   horizon  = 12,   metrics  = c("MAE", "RMSE", "MASE") )  # Per-fold metrics tibble::as_tibble(bt)  # Aggregated (mean across folds) milt_accuracy(bt)

title: Forecast Accuracy Metrics Guide
text: vignette("milt-forecasting") — fitting models obtaining forecasts vignette("milt-model-reference") — full model parameter reference vignette("milt-eda-diagnostics") — pre-modelling diagnostics

title: Model Reference Guide
text: Repeats last observed value every forecast step. Equivalent random-walk forecast. mandatory benchmark — useful model outperform . hyperparameters. package dependency.
code: air <- milt_series(AirPassengers)  naive_fct <- milt_model("naive") |>   milt_fit(air) |>   milt_forecast(horizon = 12)  plot(naive_fct)

title: Model Reference Guide
text: Repeats last full seasonal cycle. monthly data copies month previous year. strong baseline series clear seasonality. package dependency.

title: Model Reference Guide
text: Extrapolates average trend observed first last training observations. hyperparameters. package dependency.

title: Model Reference Guide
text: Wraps forecast::auto.arima(). Searches ARIMA(p,d,q)(P,D,Q) orders using AICc BIC, optional seasonal differencing stepwise search. Requires: forecast use: solid default univariate monthly quarterly series. Use stepwise = FALSE thorough search fit time concern.
code: arima_fct <- milt_model("auto_arima",                          stepwise     = TRUE,                          seasonal     = TRUE) |>   milt_fit(air) |>   milt_forecast(horizon = 24)  plot(arima_fct) tibble::as_tibble(arima_fct)

title: Model Reference Guide
text: Wraps forecast::ets(). Fits Error-Trend-Seasonality state-space model. model string "ZZZ" lets algorithm choose best combination automatically. Requires: forecast use: Robust wide range univariate series. Works well variance grows proportionally level (use "ZZZ" "MAM").
code: # Automatic selection ets_auto <- milt_model("ets") |>   milt_fit(air) |>   milt_forecast(horizon = 24)  # Force additive-error multiplicative-trend multiplicative-season (MAM) ets_mam  <- milt_model("ets", model = "MAM") |>   milt_fit(air) |>   milt_forecast(horizon = 24)

title: Model Reference Guide
text: Wraps forecast::thetaf(). Decomposes series two “theta lines” different slopes combines forecasts. Performs well M3 competition data. Requires: forecast hyperparameters (options passed ... forecast::thetaf()).

title: Model Reference Guide
text: Wraps forecast::stlf(). Decomposes series using STL (Seasonal Trend decomposition using Loess), forecasts seasonally-adjusted series ETS ARIMA, re-seasonalises. Requires: forecast
code: stl_fct <- milt_model("stl",                        method   = "ets",                        s.window = "periodic",                        robust   = TRUE) |>   milt_fit(air) |>   milt_forecast(horizon = 24)

title: Model Reference Guide
text: Wraps forecast::tbats(). Handles multiple seasonalities, Box-Cox transformations, ARMA errors, damped trends. Computationally expensive powerful complex seasonal patterns (e.g., hourly data daily weekly seasons). Requires: forecast
code: tbats_fct <- milt_model("tbats",                           use_box_cox   = NULL,                           use_trend     = NULL,                           use_arma_errors = TRUE) |>   milt_fit(air) |>   milt_forecast(horizon = 24)

title: Model Reference Guide
text: Wraps forecast::croston(). Designed intermittent demand series — many observations zero sporadic positive values. Separately smooths non-zero demand sizes intervals . Requires: forecast
code: # Simulated intermittent series set.seed(42) demand <- c(0,0,3,0,0,0,5,0,2,0,0,4,0,0,1) demand_s <- milt_series(   tibble::tibble(t = seq_along(demand), y = demand),   time_col = "t", value_cols = "y" )  croston_fct <- milt_model("croston", alpha = 0.1) |>   milt_fit(demand_s) |>   milt_forecast(horizon = 6)

title: Model Reference Guide
text: Wraps prophet::prophet(). Decomposes series trend, seasonality, holiday components. Designed business time series strong multi-seasonality irregular holidays. Requires: prophet
code: prophet_fct <- milt_model("prophet",                            yearly_seasonality      = TRUE,                            seasonality_mode        = "multiplicative",                            changepoint_prior_scale = 0.05) |>   milt_fit(air) |>   milt_forecast(horizon = 24)

title: Model Reference Guide
text: ML models convert time series supervised learning problem building lag features training window. target next-step value; features lagged values positions defined lags argument.

title: Model Reference Guide
text: Gradient-boosted decision trees. Fast, accurate, handles non-linearities interactions automatically. Requires: xgboost Feature importance available via milt_explain():
code: xgb_fct <- milt_model("xgboost",                         lags      = 1:24,                         nrounds   = 200L,                         max_depth = 6L,                         eta       = 0.05) |>   milt_fit(air) |>   milt_forecast(horizon = 12) fitted_xgb <- milt_model("xgboost", lags = 1:12) |> milt_fit(air) ex <- milt_explain(fitted_xgb, air) plot(ex)

title: Model Reference Guide
text: Microsoft’s gradient-boosting framework. Faster training lower memory use XGBoost large datasets, especially many features. Requires: lightgbm
code: lgb_fct <- milt_model("lightgbm",                         lags           = 1:12,                         num_iterations = 200L,                         learning_rate  = 0.05,                         num_leaves     = 63L) |>   milt_fit(air) |>   milt_forecast(horizon = 12)

title: Model Reference Guide
text: Ensemble decision trees via ranger. stable single trees; naturally handles interactions outliers. Requires: ranger
code: rf_fct <- milt_model("random_forest",                       lags         = 1:12,                       num.trees    = 300L,                       min.node.size = 3L) |>   milt_fit(air) |>   milt_forecast(horizon = 12)

title: Model Reference Guide
text: Linear model L1 + L2 regularisation via glmnet. Performs automatic lag selection lambda = NULL (5-fold CV). Interpretable fast. Requires: glmnet
code: # Lasso (alpha = 1) — maximum sparsity lasso_fct <- milt_model("elastic_net",                           lags  = 1:24,                           alpha = 1.0) |>   milt_fit(air) |>   milt_forecast(horizon = 12)

title: Model Reference Guide
text: Regression SVM via e1071. Works well small--medium datasets non-linear patterns. Requires: e1071
code: svm_fct <- milt_model("svm",                         lags    = 1:12,                         kernel  = "radial",                         cost    = 10,                         epsilon = 0.05) |>   milt_fit(air) |>   milt_forecast(horizon = 12)

title: Model Reference Guide
text: Non-parametric lag-based forecasting. Finds k similar historical windows averages next steps. package dependency.
code: knn_fct <- milt_model("knn",                         k       = 7L,                         lags    = 1:12,                         weights = "distance") |>   milt_fit(air) |>   milt_forecast(horizon = 12)

title: Model Reference Guide
text: deep learning backends built torch R. require torch installed Lantern C++ backend downloaded: deep learning models share training parameters:
code: milt_install_backends("deep_learning") torch::install_torch()   # run once after installing torch

title: Model Reference Guide
text: Neural Basis Expansion Analysis (Oreshkin et al. 2019). Pure multi-layer perceptron architecture stacks fully-connected blocks. block produces backcast (removing information input) forecast; block outputs summed. Achieves state---art results M4 competition without domain knowledge. Requires: torch
code: nbeats_fct <- milt_model("nbeats",                            input_chunk_length  = 24L,                            output_chunk_length = 12L,                            n_stacks            = 2L,                            hidden_size         = 128L,                            n_epochs            = 200L,                            patience            = 15L) |>   milt_fit(air) |>   milt_forecast(horizon = 24)

title: Model Reference Guide
text: Neural Hierarchical Interpolation Time Series (Challu et al. 2022). Extends N-BEATS multi-rate sampling (pooling) hierarchical interpolation, reducing computation improving long-horizon accuracy. Requires: torch
code: nhits_fct <- milt_model("nhits",                           input_chunk_length  = 36L,                           output_chunk_length = 12L,                           n_stacks            = 3L) |>   milt_fit(air) |>   milt_forecast(horizon = 24)

title: Model Reference Guide
text: Combines LSTMs multi-head attention capture short-term patterns (via LSTM encoder) long-term dependencies (via self-attention). Designed multi-horizon probabilistic forecasting. Requires: torch
code: tft_fct <- milt_model("tft",                         input_chunk_length  = 36L,                         output_chunk_length = 12L,                         hidden_size         = 128L,                         n_heads             = 4L,                         dropout             = 0.2,                         n_epochs            = 150L) |>   milt_fit(air) |>   milt_forecast(horizon = 24)

title: Model Reference Guide
text: Dilated causal convolutions allow receptive field grow exponentially depth, capturing long-range dependencies without recurrence. Requires: torch
code: tcn_fct <- milt_model("tcn",                         input_chunk_length  = 24L,                         output_chunk_length = 12L,                         n_filters           = 64L,                         n_layers            = 6L,                         dropout             = 0.1) |>   milt_fit(air) |>   milt_forecast(horizon = 12)

title: Model Reference Guide
text: Applies Vision Transformer concept time series: input window split non-overlapping patches, patch linearly embedded token, learnable positional encodings added, standard Transformer encoder processes patch tokens jointly. Excels capturing long-range dependencies efficiently. Requires: torch
code: ptst_fct <- milt_model("patch_tst",                          input_chunk_length  = 48L,                          output_chunk_length = 12L,                          patch_len           = 8L,                          d_model             = 128L,                          n_heads             = 4L,                          n_layers            = 3L) |>   milt_fit(air) |>   milt_forecast(horizon = 24)

title: Model Reference Guide
text: Probabilistic LSTM-based model trained autoregressive likelihood model. Returns Gaussian parameters (mean standard deviation) step, used construct prediction intervals analytically. Requires: torch
code: deepar_fct <- milt_model("deepar",                            input_chunk_length  = 36L,                            output_chunk_length = 12L,                            hidden_size         = 128L,                            n_layers            = 3L,                            n_epochs            = 200L) |>   milt_fit(air) |>   milt_forecast(horizon = 24)

title: Model Reference Guide
text: Use milt_compare() run rolling-origin backtest across multiple models rank accuracy:
code: models <- list(   naive  = milt_model("naive"),   arima  = milt_model("auto_arima"),   ets    = milt_model("ets"),   xgb    = milt_model("xgboost", lags = 1:12) )  cmp <- milt_compare(models, air, horizon = 12, rank_metric = "RMSE") print(cmp) plot(cmp)

title: Model Reference Guide
text: Combine fitted models weighted mean ensemble:
code: m1 <- milt_model("auto_arima") |> milt_fit(air) m2 <- milt_model("ets")        |> milt_fit(air) m3 <- milt_model("xgboost", lags = 1:12) |> milt_fit(air)  ens <- milt_ensemble(list(arima = m1, ets = m2, xgb = m3), method = "mean") ens_fct <- milt_forecast(ens, horizon = 12) plot(ens_fct)

title: Working with Multiple Series
text: milt handles multi-series data natively. grouped MiltSeries stores multiple series single object, milt_local_model() fits one model per series parallel.

title: Working with Multiple Series
code: # Build a synthetic dataset with 3 stores set.seed(42) n   <- 48L tbl <- tibble::tibble(   date     = rep(seq(as.Date("2020-01-01"), by = "month", length.out = n), 3),   store    = rep(c("A", "B", "C"), each = n),   sales    = c(     rnorm(n, 100, 10),     rnorm(n, 200, 20),     rnorm(n, 150, 15)   ) )  ms <- milt_series(tbl,                    time_col  = "date",                    value_cols = "sales",                    group_col = "store") print(ms) #> # A MiltSeries: 48 x 1 [monthly] | 3 series #> # Time range : 2020 Jan — 2023 Dec #> # Components : sales #> # Groups     : store (3 series) #> # Gaps       : none #> # A tibble: 6 × 3 #>   date       store sales #>   <date>     <chr> <dbl> #> 1 2020-01-01 A     114.  #> 2 2020-02-01 A      94.4 #> 3 2020-03-01 A     104.  #> 4 2020-04-01 A     106.  #> 5 2020-05-01 A     104.  #> 6 2020-06-01 A      98.9 #> # … with 138 more rows

title: Working with Multiple Series
Working with Multiple Series
text: milt_local_model() wraps model train independently group:
code: local_m <- milt_local_model(milt_model("ets")) fitted  <- milt_fit(local_m, ms) #> Fitting <MiltLocalModel> model… #> Local model: fitting 3 groups… #> Done in 1.71s. fct     <- milt_forecast(fitted, 12) #> Local model: generating forecasts for 3 groups. print(fct) #> # A MiltForecast <ets>: horizon = 12# Forecast from: 2023-12-01# Intervals    : 80, 95%# #> # A tibble: 6 × 7 #>   time       .model .mean .lower_80 .upper_80 .lower_95 .upper_95 #>   <date>     <chr>  <dbl>     <dbl>     <dbl>     <dbl>     <dbl> #> 1 2024-01-01 ets     99.6      84.4      115.      76.4      123. #> 2 2024-02-01 ets     99.6      84.4      115.      76.4      123. #> 3 2024-03-01 ets     99.6      84.4      115.      76.4      123. #> 4 2024-04-01 ets     99.6      84.4      115.      76.4      123. #> 5 2024-05-01 ets     99.6      84.4      115.      76.4      123. #> 6 2024-06-01 ets     99.6      84.4      115.      76.4      123. #> # … with 6 more rows plot(fct)

title: Working with Multiple Series
code: # Extract one group by filtering the underlying tibble s_A <- milt_series(   dplyr::filter(ms$as_tibble(), store == "A"),   time_col   = "date",   value_cols = "sales" )  spl <- milt_split(s_A, ratio = 0.8) cat("Train:", spl$train$n_timesteps(), "Test:", spl$test$n_timesteps(), "\n") #> Train: 38 Test: 10

title: Working with Multiple Series
code: s1 <- milt_series(AirPassengers)[1:72] s2 <- milt_series(AirPassengers)[73:144] s_full <- milt_concat(s1, s2) cat("Combined length:", s_full$n_timesteps(), "\n") #> Combined length: 144

title: Working with Multiple Series
text: Group series shape similarity:
code: # Create a list of separate MiltSeries for clustering series_list <- lapply(c("A", "B", "C"), function(g) {   milt_window(ms, group = g) })  cl <- milt_cluster(series_list, k = 2L, method = "feature_based") print(cl) #> # MiltClusters [feature_based] #> # Series: 3   Clusters: 2# Cluster sizes: #>  #> 1 2  #> 2 1

title: Working with Multiple Series
text: Train classifier labelled series:
code: # Assign synthetic labels labels <- c("high", "high", "low")   # stores A, B, C  clf <- milt_classifier("feature_based") milt_classify_fit(clf, series_list, labels)  pred <- milt_classify_predict(clf, series_list) cat("Predicted labels:", pred$labels, "\n") #> Predicted labels: high high high

title: Working with Multiple Series
text: Ensure aggregate forecasts coherent bottom-level sums:
code: # Fit models for Total + each store s_total <- milt_series(   tibble::tibble(     date  = seq(as.Date("2020-01-01"), by = "month", length.out = n),     sales = tbl$sales[tbl$store == "A"] +             tbl$sales[tbl$store == "B"] +             tbl$sales[tbl$store == "C"]   ),   time_col = "date", value_cols = "sales" )  fc_total <- milt_model("ets") |> milt_fit(s_total)  |> milt_forecast(6) #> Fitting <MiltEts> model… #> Done in 0.5s. fc_A     <- milt_model("ets") |> milt_fit(series_list[[1L]]) |> milt_forecast(6) #> Fitting <MiltEts> model… #> Done in 0.56s. fc_B     <- milt_model("ets") |> milt_fit(series_list[[2L]]) |> milt_forecast(6) #> Fitting <MiltEts> model… #> Done in 0.51s. fc_C     <- milt_model("ets") |> milt_fit(series_list[[3L]]) |> milt_forecast(6) #> Fitting <MiltEts> model… #> Done in 0.47s.  # Summing matrix (Total = A + B + C) S <- matrix(c(1,1,1, 1,0,0, 0,1,0, 0,0,1),             nrow = 4, ncol = 3, byrow = TRUE,             dimnames = list(c("Total","A","B","C"), c("A","B","C")))  rc <- milt_reconcile(   list(Total = fc_total, A = fc_A, B = fc_B, C = fc_C),   S      = S,   method = "ols" ) print(rc) #> # MiltReconciliation [ols] #> # Series: Total, A, B, C

title: milt Package Overview
text: milt stands Modern Integrated Library Timeseries. package designed provide unified R interface major families time series work: forecasting feature engineering backtesting model comparison anomaly detection changepoint analysis multi-series analysis explainability diagnostics deployment reporting package intentionally broad. Instead exposing one API classical models, another machine learning, another deep learning, uses single workflow pattern across library.

title: milt Package Overview
text: central workflow : structure applies regardless model family. makes easier : swap models without rewriting rest workflow evaluate models backtesting setup build ensembles comparisons across heterogeneous backends standardize outputs printed, plotted, converted tibbles

title: milt Package Overview
text: package centered small set reusable objects.

title: milt Package Overview
text: MiltSeries canonical container time series data inside package. user-facing functions either consume return MiltSeries. Create many source types: Common accessors:
code: air <- milt_series(AirPassengers) print(air) #> # A MiltSeries: 144 x 1 [monthly] #> # Time range : 1949 Jan — 1960 Dec #> # Components : value #> # Gaps       : none #> # A tibble: 6 × 2 #>   time       value #>   <date>     <dbl> #> 1 1949-01-01   112 #> 2 1949-02-01   118 #> 3 1949-03-01   132 #> 4 1949-04-01   129 #> 5 1949-05-01   121 #> 6 1949-06-01   135 #> # … with 138 more rows air$n_timesteps() #> [1] 144 air$freq() #> [1] "monthly" air$start_time() #> [1] "1949-01-01" air$end_time() #> [1] "1960-12-01" head(air$values()) #> [1] 112 118 132 129 121 135

code:
out       <- milt_step_scale(air, method = "zscore") air_z     <- out$series    # scaled MiltSeries scaler    <- out$step      # MiltScaleStep — keeps centering/scaling params  # Verify: mean ≈ 0, sd ≈ 1 cat("Mean:", round(mean(air_z$values()), 4), "
") #> Mean: 0 cat("SD:  ", round(sd(air_z$values()),   4), "
") #> SD:   1  # Invert air_back  <- milt_step_unscale(scaler, air_z) cat("Original max:", max(air$values()), "
") #> Original max: 622 cat("Restored max:", max(air_back$values()), "
") #> Restored max: 622

what: 7. Using features with an ML model
title: Building Pipelines with milt
code: # Scale → fit → forecast scaled_out <- milt_step_scale(air, method = "zscore") air_scaled <- scaled_out$series scaler     <- scaled_out$step  m   <- milt_model("xgboost", lags = 1:12) |> milt_fit(air_scaled) #> Fitting <MiltXGBoost> model… #> Done in 0.05s. fct <- milt_forecast(m, 12)  # Point forecasts back in original units fct_vals_orig <- scaler$inverse_transform_vector(fct$as_tibble()$.mean) cat("Forecast (original units):\n") #> Forecast (original units): print(round(fct_vals_orig, 1)) #>  [1] 443.0 417.3 444.3 463.7 532.7 563.1 611.1 606.7 550.8 461.1 417.6 440.6

title: MiltSeries API Reference
text: primary constructor. Accepts several input formats. base-R ts object (common): tibble explicit column names: Multi-series (panel) long-format tibble:
code: milt_series(   data,   time_col   = NULL,   value_cols = NULL,   group_col  = NULL,   frequency  = NULL,   metadata   = list() ) library(milt)  ap <- milt_series(AirPassengers) print(ap) library(tibble)  df <- tibble(   date  = seq(as.Date("2020-01-01"), by = "month", length.out = 36),   sales = cumsum(rnorm(36, mean = 100)) )  s <- milt_series(df, time_col = "date", value_cols = "sales", frequency = "monthly") print(s) panel <- tibble(   date   = rep(seq(as.Date("2022-01-01"), by = "day", length.out = 90), 3),   series = rep(c("A", "B", "C"), each = 90),   value  = rnorm(270) )  ms <- milt_series(panel,                   time_col  = "date",                   value_cols = "value",                   group_col = "series") print(ms)

text: Change sampling frequency series: milt_resample() arguments:
code: # Aggregate daily → weekly (mean) daily_s <- milt_series(   tibble(     date  = seq(as.Date("2023-01-01"), by = "day", length.out = 365),     value = rnorm(365)   ),   time_col   = "date",   value_cols = "value",   frequency  = "daily" )  weekly_s <- milt_resample(daily_s, frequency = "weekly", fun = mean)

code: # Insert NAs at missing time steps, then linearly interpolate filled <- milt_fill_gaps(s, method = "linear")

title: MiltSeries API Reference
text: milt supports three kinds covariates can attached series: Static covariates — scalar features apply whole series (e.g. store size, country code). Past covariates — time-varying features known forecast origin (e.g. lagged external variables). Future covariates — time-varying features known entire forecast horizon (e.g. calendar events, planned promotions). milt_add_covariates() arguments:
code: library(tibble)  ap <- milt_series(AirPassengers)  # Static: one row per series static <- tibble(region = "North America")  # Future: calendar flags over the forecast horizon future_dates <- seq(   from       = as.Date("1960-02-01"),   by         = "month",   length.out = 12 ) future_covs <- tibble(   date      = future_dates,   is_summer = lubridate::month(future_dates) %in% 6:8 )  ap <- milt_add_covariates(ap,                           static = static,                           future = future_covs)  # Retrieve attached covariates milt_get_covariates(ap, type = "static") milt_get_covariates(ap, type = "future")

title: MiltSeries API Reference
text: group_col set, milt functions automatically apply per-group.
code: # Build a 3-series dataset panel <- tibble(   month  = rep(seq(as.Date("2020-01-01"), by = "month", length.out = 60), 3),   region = rep(c("East", "West", "Central"), each = 60),   sales  = c(     cumsum(rnorm(60, 5)),     cumsum(rnorm(60, 3)),     cumsum(rnorm(60, 4))   ) )  ms <- milt_series(panel,                   time_col   = "date",                   value_cols = "sales",                   group_col  = "region",                   frequency  = "monthly")  ms$n_series()        # 3 ms$is_multi_series() # TRUE  # Fit and forecast all series at once fc <- milt_model("ets") |>   milt_fit(ms) |>   milt_forecast(h = 12)  print(fc)

what: milt - Modern Integrated Library for Timeseries
title: milt - Modern Integrated Library for Timeseries
text: milt unified R framework time series forecasting, anomaly detection, classification, clustering, diagnostics, deployment. designed give R users single, consistent interface across classical statistical models, machine learning backends, deep-learning integrations. package follows one core idea: users need learn different API model family. Whether fitting naive baseline, Auto-ARIMA model, XGBoost regressor, ensemble, workflow stays consistent. Documentation site: https://ntigideon.github.io/milt

title: milt - Modern Integrated Library for Timeseries
text: milt intended cover end--end time series workflow: series creation conversion ts, tibble, data.frame, tsibble, xts, zoo, numeric vectors forecasting classical, ML, deep-learning backends train/test splitting, rolling-origin backtesting, model comparison feature engineering lag, rolling, Fourier, calendar, scaling steps anomaly detection changepoint analysis multi-series clustering, classification, hierarchical reconciliation diagnostics, exploratory analysis, model explainability saving, loading, dashboarding, serving models

title: milt - Modern Integrated Library for Timeseries
text: milt keeps heavy backend dependencies optional core package installs quickly reliably. Use milt_install_backends() install need:
code: # install.packages("pak") pak::pak("ntiGideon/milt") library(milt)  milt_install_backends()                        # everything milt_install_backends("forecasting")           # forecast + prophet milt_install_backends("ml")                    # xgboost, lightgbm, glmnet, ranger, e1071 milt_install_backends("deep_learning")         # torch  (then run torch::install_torch()) milt_install_backends("extras")                # anomaly, clustering, causal impact milt_install_backends("reporting")             # rmarkdown, shiny, plumber milt_install_backends("prophet")               # a single package by name

title: milt - Modern Integrated Library for Timeseries
text: central workflow : shape used throughout package:
code: library(milt)  series <- milt_series(AirPassengers)  fct <- milt_model("auto_arima") |>   milt_fit(series) |>   milt_forecast(horizon = 12)  print(fct) plot(fct) milt_model("<name>") |> milt_fit(series) |> milt_forecast(horizon = 12)

title: milt - Modern Integrated Library for Timeseries
text: package organized around small set core objects: MiltSeries: canonical time series container MiltModel: fitted unfitted model object MiltForecast: forecast results point forecasts intervals MiltBacktest: rolling-origin validation results MiltComparison: ranked comparison across multiple models MiltAnomalies: anomaly detection output design keeps downstream workflows stable. plotting, printing, coercion patterns apply across outputs.

title: milt - Modern Integrated Library for Timeseries
code: library(milt)  # 1. Create a series air <- milt_series(AirPassengers)  # 2. Inspect and diagnose print(air) dx <- milt_diagnose(air) print(dx)  # 3. Fit and forecast fct <- milt_model("ets") |>   milt_fit(air) |>   milt_forecast(horizon = 24)  print(fct) plot(fct)  # 4. Evaluate on a split spl <- milt_split(air, ratio = 0.8) fct_test <- milt_model("naive") |>   milt_fit(spl$train) |>   milt_forecast(spl$test$n_timesteps())  milt_accuracy(   actual = spl$test$values(),   predicted = fct_test$as_tibble()$.mean )  # 5. Backtest a model bt <- milt_backtest(   model = milt_model("naive"),   series = air,   horizon = 12,   initial_window = 108L,   stride = 12L )  print(bt) plot(bt)

title: milt - Modern Integrated Library for Timeseries
text: milt supports direct feature steps composed pipelines. Direct feature engineering: Pipeline workflow:
code: air_features <- air |>   milt_step_lag(lags = 1:12) |>   milt_step_rolling(windows = c(3L, 6L), fns = c("mean", "sd")) |>   milt_step_fourier(period = 12, K = 2) pipe <- milt_pipeline() |>   milt_pipe_step_lag(lags = 1:12) |>   milt_pipe_step_rolling(windows = 3L, fns = "mean") |>   milt_pipe_model("xgboost")

title: Add covariates to a MiltSeries — milt_add_covariates

title: Add covariates to a MiltSeries — milt_add_covariates
text: past: time-varying covariates observed past (time range series). code: s <- milt_series(AirPassengers) dates <- s$as_tibble()$time cov_df <- data.frame(time = dates, month_num = as.integer(format(dates, "%m"))) s2 <- milt_add_covariates(s, cov_df, type = "past", time_col = "time") #> Added past covariates to <MiltSeries> (1 covariate column).

title: Walk-forward backtesting of a milt model — milt_backtest
text: model unfitted MiltModel (created milt_model()). model cloned re-fitted scratch fold's training window; original object modified. series MiltSeries object containing full time series. horizon Positive integer. title: milt (development version)
text: Core data layer milt_series() creates MiltSeries object vector, ts, data.frame, tibble. Supports univariate multivariate series, optional grouping (multi-series), covariate columns. milt_window(), milt_split(), milt_concat(), milt_resample(), milt_fill_gaps() series manipulation. Universal model interface milt_model() dispatches registered backend name string. milt_fit() trains model MiltSeries. milt_forecast() generates point forecasts prediction intervals. milt_refit() updates fitted model new data. milt_backtest() performs sliding-window cross-validation. milt_compare() runs multiple models returns ranked comparison table. milt_ensemble() combines model forecasts weighted averaging. milt_local_model() wraps model train independently group grouped MiltSeries. Classical backends (forecast package): "auto_arima", "ets", "tbats", "theta", "croston", "nnetar", "hw_additive", "hw_multiplicative". Statistical backends: "stlm", "tslm", "var". Machine-learning backends (lazy-loaded): "xgboost", "lightgbm", "random_forest", "elastic_net", "svm", "knn" (pure R, extra dependency). Deep-learning backends (requires torch reticulate + Darts): "nbeats", "nhits", "tcn", "deepar", "tft", "patch_tst". fable / tidyverts backends: "fable_arima", "fable_ets", "fable_var". Prophet backend: "prophet" (requires prophet package). Feature engineering milt_step_lag(), milt_step_roll(), milt_step_fourier(), milt_step_calendar() — ML-ready feature constructors. milt_step_scale() / milt_step_unscale() — invertible normalization (z-score, min-max, robust). Anomaly detection milt_detector() + milt_detect() universal detector API. Built-detectors: "iqr", "gesd", "grubbs", "stl", "isolation_forest", "lof", "ensemble", "autoencoder". milt_changepoints() wraps changepoint strucchange backends. Multi-series & hierarchy milt_cluster() — time series clustering (euclidean, k-Shape, feature-based, DTW k-means). milt_classifier() / milt_classify_fit() / milt_classify_predict() — feature-based ROCKET classifiers. milt_reconcile() — hierarchical forecast reconciliation (OLS, WLS-struct, MinT-shrink). Analysis milt_eda() — automated exploratory analysis ADF/KPSS stationarity tests STL seasonality strength. milt_explain() — model explainability via SHAP (xgboost), variable importance (ranger), coefficient extraction (glmnet). milt_causal_impact() — causal inference wrapper around CausalImpact. Deployment milt_save() / milt_load() — RDS-based model serialisation version metadata. milt_serve() — Plumber REST API /health, /series_info, /forecast endpoints. milt_dashboard() — four-tab Shiny monitoring dashboard. milt_report() — automated R Markdown / Quarto HTML PDF report. Infrastructure ModelRegistry — central registry; new backends register ModelRegistry$register(). milt_info(), milt_warn(), milt_abort() — cli-powered messaging utilities. milt_setup_darts() — helper configure Python / Darts environment deep-learning backends.
